title: Resistant Ovary Syndrome (Savage Syndrome): When Follicles Exist but Won't Respond
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: high FSH/LH with high AMH and low estradiol, confirmatory gonadotropin non-response, excluding POI/PCOS/hypothalamic disease and FSH-secreting adenoma, why laparoscopic ovarian drilling fails, and FSHR sequencing vs whole-exome testing
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Secondary amenorrhea — failed ovulation induction with letrozole and high-dose gonadotropins
pmh: No response to high-dose gonadotropins — follicles unresponsive
Normal-sized ovaries with antral follicles on ultrasound
medications: Prior letrozole for ovulation induction — failed

caseDescription: A 26-year-old woman with secondary amenorrhea has elevated FSH (24–30 IU/L) and LH (11–31 IU/L), high AMH (8.6 ng/mL), and low estradiol. She failed ovulation induction with letrozole and high-dose exogenous gonadotropins — no follicular growth and no estradiol rise. Ultrasound shows normal-sized ovaries with multiple small antral follicles and thin endometrium. The consolidated picture confirms Resistant Ovary Syndrome (Savage Syndrome): follicles are present but granulosa cells are insensitive to FSH/LH — FSH-receptor mutation, autoimmune receptor blockade, or post-receptor signaling defect. POI is excluded (high AMH, follicles present); PCOS is excluded (FSH abnormally high, no ovulatory response, normal-sized ovaries); hypothalamic/pituitary causes excluded (gonadotropins high, not low). Laparoscopic ovarian drilling has no role (unlike PCOS). Pituitary gonadotroph adenoma is unlikely (low E2, no ovarian hyperstimulation). Genetic confirmation: targeted FSHR sequencing first; whole-exome sequencing if broader search needed; autoimmune workup if genetics negative.
keyFindings: High FSH + high AMH + low E2 = follicles present but not functioning
Non-response to letrozole AND high-dose gonadotropins — confirmatory dynamic test
Normal-sized ovaries with antral follicles — not atrophic (POI) or polycystic enlarged (PCOS)
LOD ineffective — receptor defect, not androgen-driven follicle arrest
Pituitary adenoma unlikely — low E2, physiologic-range gonadotropins, no hyperstimulation
finalDiagnosis: Resistant Ovary Syndrome (Savage Syndrome) — confirmed by hormonal profile (elevated FSH/LH, high AMH, low estradiol) and complete lack of follicular/estradiol response to exogenous gonadotropins. Management: cyclic estrogen-progestin or HRT for endometrial protection; fertility counselling — IVF with donor oocytes most effective; autoimmune workup (anti-ovarian, anti-TPO); FSHR gene sequencing or whole-exome panel; psychological support and long-term hypoestrogenism counselling.
learningObjectives: Recognise the hormonal signature of ROS: high FSH/LH, high/normal AMH, low estradiol
Use gonadotropin stimulation failure as confirmatory and exclude POI, PCOS, and central causes
Explain why laparoscopic ovarian drilling helps PCOS but not ROS
Distinguish ROS from FSH/LH-secreting pituitary adenoma using estradiol, imaging, and ovarian morphology
Apply rational genetic testing (FSHR targeted vs WES) and autoimmune workup

items.title: Clinical snapshot: secondary amenorrhea with gonadotropin non-response
items.content: 26-year-old woman, reproductive age: persistent secondary amenorrhea. Labs: FSH 24–30 IU/L, LH 11–31 IU/L (both elevated), AMH 8.6 ng/mL (high), estradiol low. Failed letrozole ovulation induction and high-dose gonadotropins — no follicular growth, no estradiol rise. Ultrasound: normal-sized ovaries with antral follicles, thin endometrium. The decisive finding is non-response to high-dose exogenous gonadotropins — ovaries contain follicles (high AMH) but granulosa cells cannot respond to FSH/LH stimulation.
keyPoints: FSH/LH high, AMH high, E2 low.

items.title: Why high FSH, high AMH, and low estradiol define resistant ovary syndrome
items.content: The signature triad: elevated FSH (>20–25 IU/L) from pituitary overdrive when ovaries do not respond; elevated or normal LH; low estradiol (<50 pg/mL) confirming hypoestrogenism; normal or high AMH proving follicles exist in the ovary. This combination — follicles present but not functioning — is the hallmark of Savage Syndrome. It differs from POI (low AMH, atrophic ovaries, depleted follicles) and from PCOS (low/normal FSH, androgen excess, enlarged polycystic ovaries). Prolactin and TSH should be normal to exclude secondary causes.
keyPoints: High FSH + high AMH + low E2 = ROS pattern.
Follicles exist but do not function.

items.title: Why POI, PCOS, and hypothalamic causes are excluded in this case
items.content: Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI): low AMH, small/atrophic ovaries, few or no antral follicles — opposite of AMH 8.6. PCOS: FSH low or normal (not 24–30), LH often disproportionately high, enlarged ovaries with >12 follicles and thick stroma, often responds to letrozole/clomiphene or LOD — this patient has high FSH and complete stimulation failure. Hypothalamic/pituitary amenorrhea: FSH and LH are low or inappropriately normal, not elevated. Secondary amenorrhea with high gonadotropins and high AMH narrows to ROS or rare gonadotroph adenoma — the latter excluded by low E2 and normal pituitary imaging.
keyPoints: POI: low AMH, atrophic ovaries — excluded.
PCOS: low FSH, enlarged ovaries — excluded.

items.title: How gonadotropin stimulation failure confirms resistant ovary syndrome
items.content: Dynamic test: administer high-dose exogenous FSH or hMG. In ROS — no rise in estradiol and no follicular growth on serial ultrasound. This patient already has the confirmatory result: failed letrozole (oral aromatase inhibitor pathway) and failed high-dose parenteral gonadotropins. Ovarian biopsy (rarely needed today) would show normal primordial and developing follicles without luteinization or mature follicles. Progesterone challenge: no withdrawal bleeding (low estrogen). Estrogen-progesterone challenge: withdrawal bleeding occurs — confirms functional uterus and outflow tract, localising the defect to ovarian unresponsiveness.
keyPoints: High-dose FSH/hMG with no E2 rise = confirmatory.
This patient already has positive dynamic test.

items.title: How clinical confirmation criteria establish Savage syndrome
items.content: Resistant Ovary Syndrome is confirmed when all are present: (1) secondary amenorrhea; (2) high FSH (>25 IU/L on two occasions); (3) low estradiol; (4) normal or high AMH; (5) normal karyotype 46,XX; (6) no follicular or estradiol response to exogenous gonadotropins. Ultrasound supports: normal-sized ovaries with visible antral follicles, thin endometrium. Optional: FSHR mutation or anti-ovarian antibodies for aetiology. This patient meets all clinical criteria — diagnosis is confirmed before genetics return.
keyPoints: Six clinical criteria — all met here.

items.title: Why ultrasound distinguishes ROS from POI and PCOS
items.content: ROS: normal-sized ovaries with multiple small antral follicles; thin endometrium from hypoestrogenism. POI: small or atrophic ovaries, few or no follicles. PCOS: enlarged ovaries, >12 peripheral follicles, increased stromal echogenicity, often thick endometrium if unopposed estrogen. The ultrasound here supports ROS — follicles visible but not the polycystic morphology. MRI pituitary should be normal to exclude gonadotroph adenoma if any doubt remains.

items.title: FSH-receptor defect, autoimmune blockade, and post-receptor signaling failure
items.content: ROS pathophysiology: ovaries contain follicles but granulosa cells are insensitive to FSH/LH. Mechanisms include FSH-receptor (FSHR) gene loss-of-function mutations on chromosome 2p21, autoimmune blockade of FSH receptors (anti-ovarian antibodies), or rare post-receptor signaling defects. Other genes implicated in broader panels: LHR, BMP15, GDF9, NR5A1, NOBOX, FIGLA. Autoimmune ROS may coexist with thyroid autoimmunity — check anti-TPO. Karyotype excludes Turner mosaicism (should be 46,XX).
keyPoints: Autoimmune receptor blockade — WES negative subset.

items.title: Why laparoscopic ovarian drilling does not help resistant ovary syndrome
items.content: LOD is effective only in PCOS — it destroys part of the thick androgen-secreting ovarian cortex, reduces androgens, restores LH/FSH balance, and allows arrested follicles to mature. In ROS the problem is not cortical thickening or androgen excess — it is FSH-receptor or post-receptor resistance. Drilling cannot restore receptor function; follicles still will not respond to FSH even when abundant. Risks in ROS: surgical trauma, adhesions, and damage to healthy follicles in an already functionally impaired ovary. LOD is contraindicated as a treatment strategy in Savage Syndrome.
keyPoints: LOD works in PCOS — androgen-driven arrest.
ROS: receptor defect — surgery cannot fix.
LOD risks harm without benefit in ROS.

items.title: How ROS differs from PCOS for treatment decisions including LOD
items.content: Comparison table: PCOS — high AMH, low/normal FSH, high LH, normal/high E2, enlarged thick-capsule ovaries, excess androgens, LOD effective. ROS — high AMH, high FSH, elevated LH, low E2, normal-sized ovaries, receptor resistance, LOD ineffective. The resident error is applying a PCOS algorithm (clomiphene → gonadotropins → LOD) to a patient whose high FSH and gonadotropin failure already signal ROS. Once ROS is confirmed, pivot to HRT for hypoestrogenism and donor-oocyte IVF for fertility — not further ovulation induction or drilling.
keyPoints: PCOS: low FSH, androgens, LOD helps.
ROS: high FSH, low E2, LOD fails.
Do not use PCOS ladder in ROS.

items.title: How to distinguish ROS from an FSH/LH-secreting pituitary adenoma
items.content: Gonadotroph adenoma: FSH/LH often markedly high (>50–100 IU/L) or discordant; estradiol HIGH from continuous ovarian stimulation; enlarged cystic ovaries; may have hyperestrogenic symptoms, thick endometrium, irregular bleeding. ROS: FSH 24–30 (physiologic overdrive range), low E2, normal-sized ovaries, amenorrhea, hypoestrogenic, no gonadotropin response. Pituitary MRI normal in ROS; macroadenoma/microadenoma in adenoma. Check α-subunit of glycoprotein hormones (often elevated in adenoma) and inhibin B (higher in adenoma). This patient's pattern fits ROS, not adenoma.
keyPoints: Adenoma: very high gonadotropins + HIGH E2.

items.title: FSHR targeted sequencing versus whole-exome sequencing for confirmation
items.content: Targeted FSHR gene sequencing is most direct and cost-effective when genetic ROS is suspected. Whole-exome sequencing (WES) covers FSHR, LHR, BMP15, GDF9, NR5A1, NOBOX, FIGLA and other folliculogenesis genes — useful when no family history or suspected novel/compound heterozygous mutation. Limitations: WES may miss promoter/regulatory defects; autoimmune ROS will have negative genetics. If WES normal → anti-FSH receptor antibodies and anti-ovarian antibody testing. WES confirms ROS only when a pathogenic mutation is found — clinical/functional diagnosis stands regardless.
keyPoints: WES broader — confirmatory if mutation found.

items.title: How to manage resistant ovary syndrome after diagnosis
items.content: Cycle regulation and endometrial protection: combined estrogen-progestin or cyclic HRT — she is hypoestrogenic. Fertility: usually unresponsive to ovulation induction; IVF with donor oocytes is most effective; own-oocyte IVF typically fails. Autoimmune workup: anti-ovarian antibodies, anti-TPO, adrenal antibodies as indicated. Genetic study: FSHR sequencing or targeted reproductive panel/WES. Counselling: fertility prognosis, psychological support, long-term risks of estrogen deficiency (bone, cardiovascular, cognition). Do not offer LOD or repeated high-dose gonadotropin cycles without re-evaluating the diagnosis.

items.title: Final diagnosis and summary statement
items.content: 26-year-old woman with secondary amenorrhea, FSH 24–30 IU/L, LH 11–31 IU/L, AMH 8.6 ng/mL, and non-response to letrozole and high-dose gonadotropins — diagnostic of Resistant Ovary Syndrome (Savage Syndrome). Ovarian follicles are present but functionally unresponsive due to receptor or post-receptor defect. POI, PCOS, central causes, pituitary adenoma, and LOD are excluded or inappropriate. Confirm with karyotype 46,XX, autoimmune and genetic studies for aetiology; manage hypoestrogenism and counsel on donor-oocyte pathways.

items.question: Why does laparoscopic ovarian drilling fail in resistant ovary syndrome when it can restore ovulation in PCOS?
items.answer: Because the mechanisms differ entirely. PCOS follicle arrest is driven by excess androgens and a thick ovarian cortex suppressing FSH action — LOD reduces androgen production and restores the LH/FSH balance mechanically. ROS has normal-sized ovaries with intact follicles but FSH-receptor or post-receptor insensitivity — granulosa cells cannot respond to FSH regardless of androgen milieu. Drilling does not repair receptor function and risks adhesions and follicular damage without any chance of restoring ovulation.

items.question: How is resistant ovary syndrome confirmed in a young woman with secondary amenorrhea and elevated FSH?
items.answer: Document secondary amenorrhea with FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions, low estradiol, normal or high AMH, and 46,XX karyotype. Ultrasound should show normal-sized ovaries with antral follicles (not atrophic). Perform dynamic testing: high-dose exogenous FSH or hMG — absence of estradiol rise and follicular growth confirms ovarian resistance. Exclude POI (low AMH), PCOS (low FSH, polycystic morphology), and central causes (low gonadotropins). Progesterone and estrogen-progesterone withdrawal tests confirm uterine outflow function. Genetics and autoimmune studies identify aetiology but are not required for the clinical diagnosis.

items.question: Why is an FSH/LH-secreting pituitary adenoma unlikely in this patient?
items.answer: Because gonadotroph adenomas cause markedly elevated or discordant FSH/LH with high estradiol from continuous ovarian stimulation — leading to enlarged cystic ovaries, hyperestrogenic symptoms, and often irregular bleeding rather than hypoestrogenic amenorrhea. This patient has FSH in the 24–30 range (physiologic overdrive, not extreme autonomous secretion), low estradiol, thin endometrium, normal-sized ovaries, and no response to exogenous gonadotropins — the pattern of ovarian resistance, not pituitary hyperstimulation.

items.question: How should genetic testing be approached after confirming resistant ovary syndrome?
items.answer: Start with targeted FSHR gene sequencing — the most common genetic cause. If negative or if broader folliculogenesis defects are suspected, proceed to a reproductive gene panel or whole-exome sequencing covering FSHR, LHR, BMP15, GDF9, NR5A1, NOBOX, and FIGLA. If all genetic testing is negative, pursue anti-ovarian and anti-FSH-receptor antibodies and anti-TPO for autoimmune ROS. Remember: a negative WES does not overturn the clinical diagnosis established by hormones and gonadotropin non-response.

items.stem: A 26-year-old woman has secondary amenorrhea, FSH 28 IU/L, AMH 8.6 ng/mL, and low estradiol. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: High FSH + high AMH + low E2 = follicles present but not functioning — ROS. POI has low AMH; hypothalamic amenorrhea has low gonadotropins; PCOS has low/normal FSH.

items.stem: Which finding most decisively confirms resistant ovary syndrome in this patient?
options: No follicular growth or estradiol rise after high-dose gonadotropins
items.explanation: Dynamic gonadotropin stimulation failure — no E2 rise, no follicle growth — is the confirmatory functional test. This patient failed both letrozole and high-dose gonadotropins.

items.stem: A colleague suggests laparoscopic ovarian drilling for this patient after failed clomiphene and gonadotropins. What is the correct response?
options: Proceed — LOD helps all anovulatory women
Decline — LOD is for PCOS with androgen-driven arrest, not FSH-receptor resistance
Proceed only if AMH is low
LOD is first-line before gonadotropins in ROS
items.explanation: LOD reduces androgens in PCOS — ineffective and potentially harmful in ROS where the defect is receptor/post-receptor insensitivity, not cortical androgen excess.

items.stem: Which feature best excludes primary ovarian insufficiency in favour of resistant ovary syndrome?
options: AMH 8.6 ng/mL with visible antral follicles
items.explanation: POI shows depleted follicles with low AMH and atrophic ovaries. High AMH (8.6) with antral follicles proves follicular reserve is intact — the problem is resistance, not depletion.

items.stem: Which pattern suggests FSH-secreting pituitary adenoma rather than resistant ovary syndrome?
options: High FSH with low estradiol and normal-sized ovaries
High FSH with high estradiol and enlarged cystic ovaries
items.explanation: Gonadotroph adenoma → autonomous gonadotropin secretion → high E2 and ovarian hyperstimulation. ROS → high FSH/LH with low E2 and non-responsive follicles.

items.stem: Why is PCOS unlikely in this patient?
options: FSH is elevated (24–30 IU/L) with complete failure of ovulation induction including gonadotropins
items.explanation: PCOS typically has low or normal FSH, not markedly elevated FSH, and often responds to letrozole/clomiphene or LOD. High FSH plus total stimulation failure points to ROS.

items.stem: What is the most appropriate fertility approach after confirming resistant ovary syndrome?
items.explanation: Own-oocyte ovulation induction typically fails in ROS. Donor-oocyte IVF is the most effective fertility option. HRT addresses hypoestrogenism regardless of fertility plans.

items.stem: Whole-exome sequencing in suspected ROS is most useful when:
options: It replaces the need for gonadotropin stimulation testing
A pathogenic FSHR or related folliculogenesis gene mutation is identified
items.explanation: WES confirms genetic aetiology when a mutation is found — it does not replace the clinical/functional diagnosis. Negative WES suggests autoimmune or unexplained ROS.

items.statement: In resistant ovary syndrome, AMH is typically normal or high because ovarian follicles are present.
items.explanation: True. High AMH reflects follicular reserve — the distinguishing feature from POI where AMH is low despite elevated FSH.

items.statement: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is an effective treatment for resistant ovary syndrome.
items.explanation: False. LOD helps PCOS by reducing androgen production — it cannot overcome FSH-receptor resistance in ROS.

items.statement: Hypothalamic amenorrhea typically presents with elevated FSH and LH.
items.explanation: False. Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea shows low or inappropriately normal gonadotropins — opposite of this case.

items.statement: A negative whole-exome sequencing result excludes the diagnosis of resistant ovary syndrome.
items.explanation: False. ROS is diagnosed clinically by hormone pattern and gonadotropin non-response. Many cases are autoimmune or have non-coding defects WES may miss.

items.statement: Cyclic estrogen-progestin therapy is indicated for endometrial protection in hypoestrogenic ROS.
items.explanation: True. Low estradiol risks endometrial atrophy and long-term bone/cardiovascular effects — HRT is indicated for cycle regulation and protection.

items.statement: FSH-secreting pituitary adenoma typically causes low estradiol and thin endometrium.
items.explanation: False. Gonadotroph adenomas cause high estradiol from ovarian overstimulation — unlike ROS with low E2.

items.assertion: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling should not be offered to this patient with resistant ovary syndrome.
items.reason: ROS is caused by FSH-receptor or post-receptor insensitivity, not androgen excess and cortical thickening that LOD addresses in PCOS.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. LOD mechanism is irrelevant to receptor-level resistance in Savage syndrome.

items.assertion: This patient's failure to respond to high-dose gonadotropins confirms resistant ovary syndrome.
items.reason: In ROS, granulosa cells cannot respond to exogenous FSH, so estradiol does not rise and follicles do not grow despite adequate follicular reserve shown by high AMH.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Dynamic gonadotropin non-response is the decisive confirmatory functional test.
